chapter_context.summary: Pronounces a woe on the drunkards of Ephraim and the mocking rulers of Jerusalem, confronting their covenant with death, and exalting the tried cornerstone in Zion.

outline.text: Fading Crown of Ephraim and Rebuking Drunken Priests
outline.explanation: This section focuses on Fading Crown of Ephraim and Rebuking Drunken Priests (28:1-8), highlighting its theological significance and showing how it develops the central message of Isaiah.

outline.text: Precept Upon Precept; Rulers' Covenant with Death
outline.explanation: This section focuses on Precept Upon Precept; Rulers' Covenant with Death (28:9-15), highlighting its theological significance and showing how it develops the central message of Isaiah.

outline.text: Tried Cornerstone and Overflowing Scourge of Judgment
outline.explanation: This section focuses on Tried Cornerstone and Overflowing Scourge of Judgment (28:16-22), highlighting its theological significance and showing how it develops the central message of Isaiah.

outline.text: Parable of the Wise Farmer's Sowing and Threshing
outline.explanation: This section focuses on Parable of the Wise Farmer's Sowing and Threshing (28:23-29), highlighting its theological significance and showing how it develops the central message of Isaiah.

key_themes.title: Prophecy of Judgment
key_themes.description: This chapter explores key facets of prophecy of judgment, revealing how God's holy character directs both judgment and redemption.

key_themes.title: The Holy One of Israel
key_themes.description: Isaiah's signature title for God, highlighting His absolute transcendent holiness, moral purity, and unique relationship with His people.

devotional.question: Reflecting on the warnings and promises of Chapter 28, how does God's double character of absolute holiness and deep comfort speak to your life today?
How does this chapter encourage you to trust in God's promises of ultimate renewal and restoration in times of spiritual or physical exile?

cultural_historical.title: The Role of the Prophet in Israel
cultural_historical.text: Prophets in the Hebrew Bible were not just fortune-tellers, but 'covenant enforcement agents'—called to confront sin, demand social justice, and bring hope of future restoration.
